participants.name: 류수빈
participants.professional_persona: 류수빈 씨는 인문학 전공자라는 배경을 무기로 딱딱한 정보 통신기술을 말랑말랑하게 풀어내어 학생들의 만족도가 높은 교수이며, 학계의 명예나 성과 경쟁보다는 정년 보장과 개인의 여유를 챙기는 실리적인 직장 생활을 이어가고 있습니다.
participants.skills_and_expertise: 인문학적 통찰력을 바탕으로 복잡한 ICT 기술을 비전공자도 쉽게 이해할 수 있도록 풀어내는 강의 설계에 능숙합니다. 최신 디지털 도구를 활용해 예술 작품을 데이터화하고 이를 시각적으로 구현하는 실무 프로젝트를 이끕니다.
participants.career_goals_and_ambitions: 이론에만 매몰되지 않고 학생들이 졸업 후 즉시 현장에 투입될 수 있는 실무 중심의 교과목을 구축하는 데 집중하고 있습니다. 학계의 과도한 성과 경쟁에 뛰어들기보다, 적절한 업무량과 개인의 삶이 균형을 이루는 안정적인 정년 보장 상태를 유지하고자 합니다.

participants.name: 박은지
participants.entity_name: Basic Research Project Promotion Committee | Nemotron-147-f2c67afc
participants.professional_persona: 박은지 씨는 교육청의 딱딱한 지침을 학교 현장의 분위기에 맞춰 부드럽게 다듬어 전달하는 능력이 탁월한 장학관입니다. 승진이라는 경쟁보다는 후배 장학사들이 고민이 있을 때 언제든 찾아와 차 한 잔 마시며 조언을 구할 수 있는 든든한 멘토로 남기를 바랍니다.
participants.skills_and_expertise: 교육청의 지침을 학교 현장의 실정에 맞게 유연하게 다듬어 적용하는 행정 능력이 뛰어납니다. 특히 교사들의 고충을 묵묵히 들어주고 이해관계자 사이의 마찰을 부드럽게 조율하는 소통 방식에 능숙합니다.
participants.career_goals_and_ambitions: 무리하게 고위직으로 승진하는 것보다, 후배 장학사들이 언제든 편하게 찾아와 조언을 구할 수 있는 믿음직한 멘토가 되는 것을 지향합니다. 현장의 목소리가 왜곡 없이 전달되는 안정적인 교육 환경을 유지하는 데 보람을 느낍니다.
participants.representative_type: Basic Research Project Promotion Committee | Nemotron-147-f2c67afc

participants.name: 김보람
participants.professional_persona: 김보람 씨는 안산 공단의 투박한 연구소에서 전자제품 설계 도면을 그리며, 인문학적 통찰을 더해 사용자가 읽기 쉬운 기술 문서를 만드는 데 집착합니다. 복잡한 회로도 사이에서 인간의 편의성을 찾아내려 애쓰지만, 가끔은 전공과 너무 다른 현실의 괴리감에 멍하니 모니터를 바라보곤 합니다.
participants.skills_and_expertise: 전자제품 개발 과정에서 사용자 경험을 분석해 설계 도면에 구체적으로 반영하며, 복잡한 기술 명세서를 누구나 이해하기 쉬운 언어로 구조화하여 문서화하는 능력이 뛰어납니다.
participants.career_goals_and_ambitions: 전공의 경계를 허문 자신만의 전문성을 인정받아, 기술적 완성도뿐만 아니라 인간의 편의성을 극대화한 혁신 제품의 메인 설계자로 성장하고 싶어 합니다.

participants.name: 구민혁
participants.professional_persona: 구민혁 씨는 내열 플라스틱의 배합비를 최적화하는 정밀한 분석력을 갖춘 연구원이며, 전공을 살려 기술 성과를 특허법에 맞게 명문화하는 작업에 능숙합니다. 구민혁 씨는 동료들의 가설에서 논리적 허점을 날카롭게 짚어내며, 이 때문에 팀 내에서 다소 차갑고 냉소적이라는 평을 듣기도 합니다.
participants.skills_and_expertise: 고분자 화합물의 열변형 특성을 분석해 산업용 내열 플라스틱의 배합비를 최적화하는 능력이 탁월합니다. 기술적인 연구 성과를 특허법에 맞게 명문화하여 권리화하는 문서 작업에 능숙하며, 동료의 가설에서 논리적 허점을 정확하게 짚어내어 때로는 냉소적이라는 평가를 받기도 합니다.
participants.career_goals_and_ambitions: 현재 공정에 적용된 플라스틱 소재를 생분해성 소재로 전환하는 프로젝트의 실질적인 기술 가이드라인을 완성하려 합니다. 무리한 직급 상승보다는 업계에서 인정받는 기술 고문으로서의 입지를 다지며, 환경 규제 변화에 유연하게 대응하는 내부 시스템을 구축하는 것에 집중합니다.

participants.name: 이상근
participants.entity_name: Basic Research Project Promotion Committee | Nemotron-103-683ae62d
participants.professional_persona: 이상근 씨는 대학 강의실과 공학 연구실을 오가며 빌딩 정보 모델링 기술로 시공 오차를 잡아내고, 중소기업 현장에서 바로 쓸 수 있는 실무 공정 매뉴얼을 집필하는 데 매진합니다.
participants.skills_and_expertise: 빌딩 정보 모델링(BIM)을 활용한 시공 오차 분석에 능숙하며, 실제 제조 현장의 도면을 최적화해 공정 시간을 단축하는 구체적인 방안을 제시합니다.
participants.career_goals_and_ambitions: 이론적인 강의를 넘어 중소 제조 기업들이 현장에서 즉시 적용해 불량률을 낮출 수 있는 실무 맞춤형 공정 매뉴얼을 출판하려 합니다.
participants.representative_type: Basic Research Project Promotion Committee | Nemotron-103-683ae62d

initial_posts.narrative: As an evaluation panel member, I see the budget as straightforward: 200 fellows × 130 million KRW each = 26 billion KRW annually. The 30% regional quota means 60 slots go to regional institutions, which aligns with the policy's goal of balanced development. I base this on the policy context stating 'approximately 200 fellows selected annually' and 'annual research grant of approximately 130 million KRW per fellow.' The direct vs. indirect cost split is 100M direct and 30M indirect per fellow, so about 77% direct and 23% indirect. This is my independent estimate; no other posts to engage with.
evidence: Policy context states 'approximately 200 fellows selected annually' and 'annual research grant of approximately 130 million KRW per fellow'.
30% regional quota is explicitly stated in policy context as '30% regional allocation quota reserved for researchers in regional institutions'.
Direct cost of 100M and indirect cost of 30M per fellow are given, so direct cost proportion is 100/130 ≈ 77%.

initial_posts.narrative: As a graduation expectancy applicant at a regional institution in Ansan, I see the fellowship as a rare opportunity for researchers like me who lack the prestige of Seoul-based labs. The policy explicitly states 200 fellows selected annually, so that's fixed. Given the 30% regional quota, I expect strong interest from regional researchers, but also from Seoul-based applicants, leading to around 1200 applications total. This yields a 16.7% acceptance rate, which feels competitive but plausible for a prestigious fellowship. For midterm evaluations, the policy emphasizes process-oriented faithful performance, not just publication counts. I think most fellows will pass, but some may struggle with lab changes or research continuity. The policy allows one lab change per year, and I estimate about 10% of fellows might change labs during the first 3 years, potentially affecting their evaluation. I'll assume 90% pass rate, so 180 pass. My perspective as a regional applicant makes me sensitive to the quota's impact on competition and the importance of stable research environments for passing evaluations.
evidence: Policy states 'approximately 200 fellows selected annually'.
30% regional quota likely increases total applications from regional institutions, but overall competition remains high.
Based on typical fellowship programs, a 6:1 application-to-selection ratio is reasonable, giving 1200 applications.
Midterm evaluation criteria focus on 'faithful performance' and process, not just outputs, so most fellows should pass.
Policy allows one lab change per year; I estimate ~10% of fellows change labs in the first 3 years, which could slightly lower pass rates.
My experience as a regional researcher suggests that regional applicants may face additional challenges in research environment stability.

initial_posts.narrative: As a member of the Basic Research Project Promotion Committee, I base my estimates on the policy's explicit stipulations and the committee's role in overseeing the selection process. The policy clearly states 200 fellows selected annually, so that number is fixed. For applications, I consider the program's prestige and the 30% regional quota, which attracts strong interest from both Seoul and regional researchers. I estimate around 1200 applications, yielding a 16.7% acceptance rate, which is competitive but plausible for a national fellowship. This aligns with 김보람's estimate, and I agree with her reasoning about the quota driving interest. For midterm evaluations, the policy emphasizes process-oriented faithful performance, not just publications. I expect most fellows to pass, but some may face challenges like lab changes or research continuity issues. The policy allows one lab change per year, and I estimate about 10% of fellows might change labs during the first 3 years, potentially affecting their evaluation. I assume a 90% pass rate, so 180 pass. My perspective as a committee member focuses on ensuring the selection and evaluation processes are fair and aligned with policy goals, which supports these estimates.

initial_posts.narrative: As a member of the Basic Research Project Promotion Committee, I base my estimates on the policy's explicit stipulations and the committee's role in overseeing the selection process. The policy clearly states 200 fellows selected annually, so that number is fixed. For applications, I consider the program's prestige and the 30% regional quota, which attracts strong interest from both Seoul and regional researchers. I estimate around 1200 applications, yielding a 16.7% acceptance rate, which is competitive but plausible for a national fellowship. This aligns with 김보람's estimate, and I agree with her reasoning about the quota driving interest. For midterm evaluations, the policy emphasizes process-oriented faithful performance, not just publications. I expect most fellows to pass, but some may face challenges like lab changes or research continuity issues. The policy allows one lab change per year, and I estimate about 10% of fellows might change labs during the first 3 years, potentially affecting their evaluation. I assume a 90% pass rate, so 180 pass. My perspective as a committee member focuses on ensuring the selection and evaluation processes are fair and aligned with policy goals, which supports these estimates.

initial_posts.narrative: As an evaluation panel member with a humanities background, I view the fellowship's selection process as highly competitive but grounded in clear policy stipulations. The policy explicitly states 200 fellows selected annually, so that number is fixed. For applications, I estimate around 1000, slightly lower than the 1200 others suggest, because the eligibility criteria (domestic postdocs within 7 years of PhD or under 39, non-tenure-track) narrow the pool significantly, and the 30% regional quota may discourage some Seoul-based applicants who perceive lower chances. This yields a 20% acceptance rate, still competitive but plausible. For midterm evaluations, the policy emphasizes process-oriented faithful performance, not just publications. I expect about 15% of fellows to face challenges such as lab changes (allowed once per year, but I estimate 10% change labs within 3 years, potentially disrupting continuity) or insufficient progress, leading to a pass rate of 85%, so 170 pass. My estimate is slightly lower than the 180 others propose because I consider the risk of research discontinuity more significant, especially for fellows who change labs or struggle with independent research. The regional quota may also affect evaluation outcomes if regional institutions have less robust research environments, though the policy aims to mitigate this.

initial_posts.narrative: As a graduation expectancy applicant with a background in optimizing polymer formulations, I view the fellowship's selection process through a lens of precision and realism. The policy explicitly states 200 fellows selected annually, so that number is fixed. For applications, I estimate around 900, lower than the 1000-1200 others suggest. My reasoning: the eligibility criteria (domestic postdocs within 7 years of PhD or under 39, non-tenure-track) significantly narrow the pool. Many potential applicants may be discouraged by the requirement to secure a host institution and complete a detailed research plan, especially those in less competitive fields. The 30% regional quota might also reduce applications from Seoul-based researchers who perceive lower chances. This yields an acceptance rate of about 22%, which is competitive but plausible. For midterm evaluations, the policy emphasizes process-oriented faithful performance. I expect about 20% of fellows to face challenges such as lab changes (allowed once per year, but I estimate 10-15% change labs within 3 years, potentially disrupting continuity) or insufficient progress, leading to a pass rate of 80%, so 160 pass. My estimate is lower than the 170-180 others propose because I consider the risk of research discontinuity more significant, especially for fellows who change labs or struggle with independent research. The regional quota may also affect evaluation outcomes if regional institutions have less robust research environments, though the policy aims to mitigate this. I disagree with 김보람 and 박은지's estimate of 1200 applications because I believe the eligibility criteria are more restrictive than they assume, and the application process is burdensome. I also disagree with 류수빈's estimate of 170 passing because I think the midterm evaluation is more rigorous than she assumes, and the risk of lab changes is higher.

refined_posts.narrative: As a graduation expectancy applicant from a regional institution in Ansan, I see the 2021-2022 window as the early phase for the first cohort of 200 fellows. Each fellow is still setting up their lab and adjusting to independent research, so productivity is modest but boosted by substantial funding. I estimate about 0.925 SCI(E) publications per fellow per year, factoring in the 30% regional quota (60 fellows in regional institutions with weaker infrastructure) and the risk of lab changes (10-15% of fellows change labs within the first 3 years, disrupting continuity). This gives 200 fellows × 2 years × 0.925 = 370 publications. My estimate is a compromise between the bottom-up 340 and top-down 400, as I believe the funding and mentoring will raise productivity above the most pessimistic view but not to the top-down's optimistic level. For tenure-track transition, I estimate 20% within 2 years post-fellowship, a midpoint between the bottom-up 25% and top-down 12.5%, because the academic job market is highly competitive and regional fellows face additional barriers, but the program's prestige and support provide an advantage. The regional quota is fully enforced, so regional participation is exactly 30%.
evidence: The 2021-2022 window covers only the first two years of the program, so only the first cohort of 200 fellows is active.
Each fellow is still in the early stage of their fellowship, with lab setup and adjustment time reducing initial publication output.

Lab changes (estimated 10-15% of fellows within first 3 years) disrupt research continuity and reduce publication output.
The generous funding (130M KRW/year) and mentoring support should increase productivity compared to typical early-career programs, justifying a midpoint estimate.
Tenure-track transition rates for similar programs are around 20-30% within 2 years; 20% is a reasonable midpoint given the competitive market and regional barriers.
The regional quota is a policy mandate, so full compliance is expected, resulting in exactly 30% regional participation.
refined_posts.judgment: The bottom-up analysis (340 publications, 25% transition) is more realistic for this early phase because it accounts for the first cohort's ramp-up challenges, lab changes, and weaker regional infrastructure. However, the top-down analysis (400 publications, 12.5% transition) correctly emphasizes the generous funding (130M KRW/year) and mentoring, which should boost productivity above the most pessimistic estimates. I synthesize a midpoint of 370 publications (0.925 per fellow per year) to balance these factors. For tenure-track transition, the bottom-up 25% is too optimistic given the competitive market and regional barriers, while the top-down 12.5% is too low given the program's prestige and support; 20% is a reasonable compromise. The regional quota is fixed at 30% by policy, so both analyses agree.

refined_posts.narrative: As an evaluation panel member with a humanities background, I synthesize the bottom-up and top-down analyses by considering the realistic conditions of the first cohort. The bottom-up estimate of 340 publications is too conservative because it underestimates the impact of the generous 130M KRW/year funding and mentoring, which can boost productivity even in early stages. The top-down estimate of 400 publications is too optimistic because it assumes typical postdoc productivity (2 papers/year) without accounting for ramp-up time, lab changes (10-15%), and weaker infrastructure at regional institutions (30% quota). I adopt 김보람's compromise of 370 publications (0.925 per fellow per year) as it reflects a realistic middle ground: 200 fellows × 2 years × 0.925 = 370. For tenure-track transition, the bottom-up 25% is too high given the competitive job market and regional barriers, while the top-down 12.5% is too low because the program's prestige and support provide an advantage. I choose 20% as a reasonable midpoint, consistent with the need for 20 tenure-track fellows from the first cohort. The regional quota is a policy mandate, so I expect full compliance at 30%.

The top-down analysis (400 publications, 12.5% transition rate) is driven by aspirational downstream targets and assumes high productivity (2 papers/fellow/year) that is typical for postdocs but may be optimistic for the first cohort adjusting to independence. The prior participant 김보람's compromise of 370 publications (0.925 per fellow per year) and 20% transition rate best balances these factors: it accounts for early-stage friction while recognizing the program's support, and it aligns with the consensus on 160 stage-two fellows (80% pass rate) from the Outputs phase, which implies a productive cohort. The regional quota of 30% is a policy mandate and is fully implemented, as both analyses agree. My humanities perspective values process and stability, leading me to favor a moderate estimate that avoids both extremes.

forward_pass.phase_summary: The group focused on the early-stage challenges of the first cohort of 200 fellows, emphasizing that ramp-up time, lab changes (10-15% within 3 years), and weaker infrastructure at regional institutions (30% quota) would limit initial SCI(E) publication output. initial_posts.narrative: As a member of the Basic Research Project Promotion Committee, I see the fellowship's long-term impact through a lens of cautious optimism. The 30% regional quota ensures some capacity building outside Seoul, but the academic job market remains tough. From the Outcomes phase, only 25% of fellows transition to tenure-track within 2 years post-fellowship, and many of those are in Seoul. By 5–10 years, I estimate about 10% of the original 200 fellows (20) will hold tenure-track positions, given attrition and competition. The regional quota's effect on publication share is modest: with 60 regional fellows producing at 0.7 publications/year vs. 140 Seoul fellows at 1.0/year, the regional share might grow by 2.5 percentage points over a decade, but systemic barriers persist. Independent PI count is higher than tenure-track because some fellows lead projects as non-tenure-track PIs; I estimate 30 such positions from the cohort, reflecting the program's support for research independence even without permanent jobs.
evidence: Tenure-track transition rate of 25% from Outcomes phase, but only a fraction (about 40%) of those transitions are to permanent positions within 5–10 years, yielding ~20 former fellows.

initial_posts.narrative: As a member of the Basic Research Project Promotion Committee, I reason backward from the forward-predicted Impact values (20 tenure-track fellows, 30 independent PIs, 2.5% regional publication share growth) and the prior phases' estimates (400 SCI(E) publications, 12.5% tenure-track transition rate, 30% regional participation, 200 active fellows, 140 stage-two fellows, 400 annual reports, 600 applications, 200 selected, 140 midterm passes). For these downstream outcomes to be achievable, the Inputs phase must provide the necessary resources and structural conditions. The program selects 200 fellows per year, so fellowship_slots must be 200. The 30% regional quota is a policy mandate, so regional_quota_pct must be 30.0%. The total annual budget must support 200 fellows at 130 million KRW each (direct cost 100M + indirect cost 30M), yielding 26 billion KRW. This budget covers both direct research costs (100M per fellow, 20 billion total) and indirect costs (30M per fellow, 6 billion total). The budget must be sufficient to maintain the program's scale and quality, as any reduction would compromise the downstream outcomes. I note that no other participants have posted yet, so I base my estimates solely on my expertise in research funding and program management. The 30% regional quota ensures that 60 regional fellows are supported, which is critical for the 2.5% regional publication share growth. The budget of 26 billion KRW is plausible given MSIT's typical funding for such programs and the policy context stating an annual budget for about 200 fellows.

initial_posts.narrative: As a postdoctoral researcher in a regional institution, I see the budget and slot allocation as critical for my own chances. The policy states 200 fellows per year at 130M KRW each, so the total annual budget is 26 billion KRW. The 30% regional quota means 60 slots are reserved for researchers like me at regional institutions, which is a lifeline for us outside the Seoul metropolitan area. The budget split is 100M direct research costs and 30M indirect costs per fellow, so about 77% goes to direct research and 23% to indirect costs. This matches the policy's resource logic.

30% of 200 slots = 60 slots reserved for regional institutions.
Direct cost 100M / total 130M ≈ 76.9% direct, 23.1% indirect.
Policy explicitly states 130M KRW per fellow per year and 30% regional quota.

initial_posts.narrative: As an evaluation panel member, I estimate that the annual call will attract about 1200 applications, reflecting strong interest among eligible early-career researchers. The policy explicitly states 200 fellows selected annually, so the acceptance rate is roughly 16.7% (200/1200). For the midterm evaluation, I expect about 170 fellows to pass (85% pass rate), considering that the evaluation is process-oriented and focuses on faithful performance rather than groundbreaking results. I anticipate that around 10-15 fellows may change host labs during the first 3 years, given the policy allows one change per year, but most will stay due to stability concerns. The evaluation panel uses criteria such as research progress, adherence to the research plan, mentoring engagement, and institutional support.

Lab changes are allowed once per year but require approval; most fellows will not change due to research continuity concerns.

initial_posts.narrative: As a postdoctoral researcher at a regional institution, I estimate that during 2021-2022, the active cohort consists of the first two annual intakes (2021 and 2022 cohorts), totaling about 400 fellows. However, the 2022 cohort starts mid-2022, so their active period is only about 10 months. Realistically, the full-year equivalent active fellows is around 300-350. Given my experience in polymer chemistry, where publication cycles are 6-12 months, I expect each fellow to produce about 1 SCI(E) publication per year on average, considering the ramp-up time for new labs and the midterm evaluation pressure. Thus, 340 publications in 2021-2022 seems reasonable. The tenure-track transition rate is low because most fellows are still in the program during this early window; only a few who leave early or secure positions before completion would count. I estimate 15% based on typical postdoc-to-faculty transition rates in Korea. The regional quota is fully met at 30% because the policy enforces it strictly, and regional institutions are eager to host fellows.
evidence: The policy selects 200 fellows per year starting in 2021, so by end of 2022, two cohorts (400 fellows) have been selected, but the 2022 cohort is active for only part of the year, reducing the full-year equivalent to about 300-350.

As a polymer chemist, I know that first-year fellows need time to set up experiments and publish, so average output is about 1 SCI(E) paper per fellow per year, leading to ~340 publications in the 2-year window.
Tenure-track transitions typically take 2-3 years after PhD; most fellows are still in the program during 2021-2022, so only a small fraction (around 15%) would have secured such positions.
The 30% regional quota is a binding policy requirement, and regional institutions actively recruit to fill those slots, so participation matches the quota exactly.
My own experience at a regional institution confirms that the quota is taken seriously and that publication output is modest in the early years due to lab setup and mentoring demands.

initial_posts.narrative: As a graduation expectancy applicant in a regional institution in Ansan, I see the long-term impact of the fellowship through a cautious lens. The 15% tenure-track transition rate from the outcomes phase suggests that only about 30 of the 200 fellows per cohort will secure tenure-track positions within 5-10 years, but many may leave academia or take non-tenure roles. Given the 5-year fellowship period and the 2021-2022 window, only the earliest cohorts (2021 and 2022) have had time to transition, so I estimate about 15 former fellows in tenure-track positions by 2026-2032 (5-10 years after start). The regional quota ensures 30% of fellows are in regional institutions, but these researchers face higher indirect costs and weaker networks, so their publication share growth is modest—maybe a 3 percentage-point increase over a decade as they gradually build capacity. For independent PI count, I estimate 50 former fellows leading independent groups: this includes those who secured tenure-track positions (15) plus those who became research professors or project PIs at regional institutions (about 35), reflecting the program's goal of fostering independence but tempered by the competitive academic job market. My estimates are lower than some might expect because the regional context and early-career challenges limit rapid advancement.
evidence: The 15% tenure-track transition rate from the outcomes phase implies about 30 fellows per cohort transition, but only the 2021 and 2022 cohorts are relevant for the 5-10 year window, yielding roughly 15 former fellows in tenure-track positions.

Regional institutions have higher indirect costs (30M vs. 100M direct) and weaker mentoring, which slows research capacity growth; a 3 percentage-point increase in regional publication share over a decade is plausible given the 60 regional fellows per cohort.

Independent PI count includes both tenure-track faculty and non-tenure-track research group leaders; based on the policy's continued support for research expenses (up to 100M KRW) for those moving to research-continuable institutions, I estimate 50 former fellows leading independent projects.
My own experience in a regional lab shows that stable funding helps but does not guarantee independence; many fellows may stay as senior postdocs rather than become PIs.
The policy's theory of change emphasizes career stability and mobility, but the academic job market in Korea is tight, especially outside Seoul, limiting the number of independent PI positions.

initial_posts.narrative: As a postdoctoral researcher with a sharp eye for logical consistency, I reason backward from the downstream targets: 15 tenure-track placements, 50 independent PIs, and 3% regional publication share growth. The 2021-2022 window covers the first two cohorts, totaling about 400 fellows. To achieve 50 independent PIs and 15 tenure-track placements, a transition rate of about 12.5% is required, which is plausible given the program's resources. The annual budget must support 200 fellows at 130 million KRW each, totaling 26 billion KRW per year. The 30% regional quota is essential to drive the 3% regional publication share growth, as it ensures proportional distribution of fellows to regional institutions. I note that the budget breakdown (direct vs. indirect costs) is already specified in the policy context: 100M direct + 30M indirect per fellow. My estimates align with the policy's stated scale and the need for a competitive selection process to ensure high-quality candidates.

initial_posts.narrative: As an evaluation panel member with a humanities background, I reason backward from the predicted Impact values (15 tenure-track placements, 50 independent PIs, 3% regional publication share growth) to what must hold at the Activities phase. The 2021-2022 window covers the first two cohorts, totaling about 400 fellows. To achieve 50 independent PIs and 15 tenure-track placements, a transition rate of about 12.5% is needed, which requires a sufficiently large and productive cohort. I estimate that about 1200 applications must be received to select 200 fellows annually, reflecting a competitive acceptance rate of about 16.7% (200/1200). This level of competition is necessary to ensure high-quality candidates who can produce the required publication output (600 SCI(E) papers) and achieve the transition rates. For midterm evaluations, I expect 280 fellows to pass (70% of 400), consistent with a 30% attrition rate typical for rigorous stage gates. The evaluation panel uses criteria such as research progress, publication output, mentoring engagement, and alignment with program goals to decide continuation. I disagree with the notion that acceptance rates could be higher; a 16.7% rate is realistic given the need for selectivity to meet downstream targets. My experience in evaluation shows that selection criteria and mentoring quality are critical for such outcomes.

initial_posts.narrative: Given the forward prediction of 15 former fellows in tenure-track positions, 3% regional publication share growth, and 50 independent PIs, I reason backward that these outcomes require a strong foundation at the Impact phase. The fellowship must have successfully supported early-career researchers to achieve independence and regional capacity. The 30% regional quota likely contributed to the 3% growth, but only if regional institutions had adequate infrastructure and mentoring. The 15 tenure-track placements and 50 independent PIs suggest that about 30-40% of the active cohort (roughly 200-400 fellows in the 2021-2022 window) transitioned to stable positions, which is ambitious but plausible with the program's resources. I base this on my experience in educational administration, where structured support and clear pathways are critical for success.

initial_posts.narrative: As an evaluation panel member, I see these Impact values as requiring a robust selection and mentoring system. For 15 former fellows to secure tenure-track positions and 50 to become independent PIs within the 2021-2022 window (active cohort ~200-400), the program must have prioritized candidates with strong research potential and provided effective mentoring. The 3% regional publication share growth indicates that regional institutions had sufficient infrastructure and support, likely due to the 30% quota. I disagree with the assumption that 30-40% transition rate is plausible; given typical academic job market constraints, a 10-15% rate is more realistic, implying the program's resources were highly leveraged. The forward prediction seems optimistic but achievable if the program focused on quality over quantity.
evidence: The 2021-2022 window covers only 1-2 cohorts, so the active fellow pool is about 200-400, not 1,000.
Tenure-track positions are scarce; a 10-15% transition rate (15 out of 200-400) is ambitious but plausible with strong mentoring.
Regional publication share growth of 3% requires sustained investment in regional infrastructure, which the 30% quota supports.
Independent PI count of 50 suggests that about 12-25% of the cohort achieved independence, consistent with effective program design.
My experience in evaluation shows that selection criteria and mentoring quality are critical for such outcomes.

fwd_bwd_fwd_bwd_pass.phase_summary: The group discussed that achieving the Impact outcomes—15 former fellows in tenure-track, 3% regional publication share growth, and 50 independent PIs—requires a strong fellowship foundation, with the 30% regional quota and structured mentoring being key causal mechanisms. Consensus emerged that the 2021-2022 window covers an active cohort of roughly 200-400 fellows, and that the program's resources (annual budget of ~130M KRW per fellow for 200 fellows) make the predicted transition rate of 30-40% plausible. A point of disagreement arose: one participant argued that a 10-15% transition rate is more realistic due to academic job market constraints, suggesting the forward predictions are optimistic but achievable with high-quality selection and mentoring. Numeric predictions were unanimous at 15, 3.0%, and 50.
